name: small brown pot
shortDescription: A small brown pot with a plain fired-clay body and a practical little mouth for herbs, salt, lamp oil, or kitchen odds and ends. It is the kind of humble vessel that vanishes into daily use, which is exactly why households keep buying more of them.
flavorText: “Small pots hold the things people notice only when they are gone.”

name: small white pot
shortDescription: A small white pot with a pale glaze, neat profile, and enough brightness to make its contents look cleaner than they probably are. Healers like it for salves, cooks like it for spices, and tidy people like it because it makes shelves look disciplined.
flavorText: “White clay turns even a pinch of salt into a presentation.”

name: large sack
shortDescription: A large sack stitched for grain, ore, wool, dried roots, or any other cargo that comes in stubborn quantities. Its value is simple: if a merchant can fill it, tie it, drag it, and count it, the sack has already earned its keep.
flavorText: “A full sack is a promise with rope around its neck.”

name: medium sack
shortDescription: A medium sack with strong seams and a carryable size, favored by caravans that measure profit in manageable loads. It is not grand, but it is trustworthy enough for beans, ore bits, flour, charcoal, and every trade good that hates loose handling.
flavorText: “The best size is the one one porter can lift and two can argue over.”

name: small sack
shortDescription: A small sack made for samples, coin bundles, rare powders, seed, or the last handfuls left after honest measuring. Merchants use them when a box is too proud and a pocket is too suspicious.
flavorText: “Small sacks are where leftovers become inventory.”

name: iron ingot
shortDescription: A bar of worked iron, plain, heavy, and ready for the forge. Smiths buy it without poetry, wagoners complain about moving it without success, and towns depend on it for hinges, tools, nails, blades, and every other useful thing that starts as stubborn metal.
flavorText: “Iron becomes whatever civilization needs after enough heat and hammering.”

name: lead ingot
shortDescription: A dull lead ingot with a soft weight and a practical reputation. It serves roofers, seal-makers, weightsmiths, alchemists, and anyone who needs metal that bends before it breaks and never pretends to be pretty.
flavorText: “Lead is not admired. It is used, which is often more profitable.”

name: copper ingot
shortDescription: A warm copper ingot with reddish shine and reliable trade value, prized by smiths, tinkers, coiners, and makers of pots, fittings, and wires. It is softer than iron, prettier than lead, and useful enough to make buyers forgive both facts.
flavorText: “Copper is the metal of bells, pans, roofs, and honest middle profits.”

name: notable tin ingot
shortDescription: A notable tin ingot, pale and clean enough to interest alloy-workers before the crate is fully open. Alone it is modest, but in the right furnace it gives bronze its voice and lets careful smiths charge for more than plain usefulness.
flavorText: “Tin rarely boasts, but bronze remembers who helped.”

name: scarce silver ingot
shortDescription: A scarce silver ingot with a cool sheen that attracts jewelers, mints, temple buyers, and nervous nobles who prefer wealth in a form that stacks neatly. It is easier to trust than rumor and easier to lose than a castle.
flavorText: “Silver buys silence, sermons, and sometimes both from the same person.”

name: scarce plazer ingot
shortDescription: A scarce plazer ingot with an unusual luster that makes ordinary smiths cautious and specialist buyers suddenly attentive. Its exact virtues depend on who is speaking, but everyone agrees it should be weighed twice and sold in good light.
flavorText: “Rare metals collect rare explanations.”

name: scarce nova ingot
shortDescription: A scarce nova ingot, bright along the edges as if it remembers a hotter birthplace. Artificers and ambitious smiths prize it for experimental alloys, while cautious merchants price it high enough to discourage casual curiosity.
flavorText: “Some metals look mined. Nova looks recovered from a falling star’s bad mood.”

name: fabled gold ingot
shortDescription: A fabled gold ingot with enough weight and shine to turn a quiet room into a negotiation chamber. Mints, nobles, temples, and thieves all understand it immediately, which makes moving it both easy to value and difficult to relax around.
flavorText: “Gold is honest about one thing: everyone wants it.”

name: iron ore
shortDescription: A rough lump of iron ore, still dirty from the earth and full of future hinges, nails, blades, and plow teeth. It is cheaper than ingots, heavier than excuses, and valuable to anyone with fuel, patience, and a proper furnace.
flavorText: “Ore is metal before discipline.”

name: lead ore
shortDescription: A heavy piece of lead ore with a dull color and a practical destiny. Miners sell it by weight, smelters judge it by yield, and merchants learn quickly that soft metals can still make very hard work.
flavorText: “Lead begins as an ugly stone and somehow becomes useful without becoming charming.”

name: copper ore
shortDescription: Copper ore with earthy stone and hints of green-blue promise in its cracks. It is raw trade stock for smelters and foundries, less glamorous than finished metal but often more profitable when bought from tired miners at the right hour.
flavorText: “The color in the stone is a whisper of kettles, coins, and roofs.”

name: tin ore
shortDescription: Tin ore that looks plain until a trained buyer notices the useful pale glint within. It travels from mine carts to smelters to alloy shops, where modest stone helps create metalwork that sounds stronger than its ingredients.
flavorText: “Tin is the quiet partner in many loud tools.”

name: silver ore
shortDescription: Silver ore with dark stone wrapped around a clean metallic promise. Prospectors brag about it, refiners distrust the bragging, and merchants prefer to buy it after someone else has done at least a little honest testing.
flavorText: “Silver in the rock is wealth wearing a miner’s patience.”

name: scarce nova ore
shortDescription: Scarce nova ore, raw and strange, marked by bright flecks that make torchlight behave oddly. It draws artificers, speculative investors, and miners who swear the seam hummed at night, though none agree what that should do to the price.
flavorText: “The ore does not glow enough to be useful, only enough to be expensive.”

name: scarce plazer ore
shortDescription: Scarce plazer ore with a rare metallic shimmer buried in stubborn stone. It requires skill to refine and nerve to buy untested, but the right buyer will pay handsomely for the chance that the furnace reveals something remarkable.
flavorText: “Every rare ore is half mineral, half gamble.”

name: scarce gold ore
shortDescription: Scarce gold ore, plain enough at a distance and very interesting up close. It carries the dangerous promise of coin before coin exists, which makes miners secretive, guards alert, and merchants suddenly excellent at counting sacks.
flavorText: “Gold in stone is still gold to anyone patient enough to crush the stone.”

name: basalt rocks
shortDescription: Dark basalt rocks with a hard volcanic character and a useful weight. Builders value them for roads, walls, and foundations, while sculptors complain about their stubbornness right up until a patron praises the finished strength.
flavorText: “Basalt is what fire leaves behind when it decides to become dependable.”

name: clay rocks
shortDescription: Clay rocks that break down into the useful earth of potters, brickmakers, and plasterers. They look humble in a wagon, but every kiln town knows these lumps can become jars, tiles, cups, walls, and steady income.
flavorText: “Clay begins rough so civilization can smooth it later.”

name: diorite rocks
shortDescription: Diorite rocks with speckled toughness and a builder’s reputation. They are awkward to move, hard to shape, and excellent at becoming the sort of wall or floor that outlasts whoever complained about the labor cost.
flavorText: “Diorite does not care who owns the building.”

name: granite rocks
shortDescription: Granite rocks, heavy, enduring, and fit for serious construction or stubborn sculpture. Masons prize them for thresholds, pillars, and memorials because granite accepts effort slowly and then spends centuries refusing to apologize.
flavorText: “Granite is patience made heavy.”

name: limestone boulder
shortDescription: A limestone boulder pale with quarry dust and full of architectural possibility. It can become blocks, lime, carvings, or respectable public buildings, provided someone first solves the problem of moving a very large promise.
flavorText: “Every fine wall starts as a rock that refused to be convenient.”

name: limestone pebbles
shortDescription: Limestone pebbles, small enough for sacks but useful in bulk for paths, mortar work, drainage, and workshop mixtures. They do not inspire songs, yet road builders and lime burners buy them with the quiet devotion of practical people.
flavorText: “Pebbles are just boulders that learned to travel.”

name: limestone rocks
shortDescription: Limestone rocks with a pale, workable grain that makes them welcome in yards, kilns, and mason shops. They are common enough to haggle over and useful enough that the haggling usually ends with a wagon being loaded anyway.
flavorText: “Limestone is ordinary until a city needs mortar.”

name: marble rock
shortDescription: A raw marble rock with veining hidden beneath dust and rough cuts. Sculptors see figures waiting inside it, builders see noble halls, and merchants see freight fees large enough to make beauty feel heavier than expected.
flavorText: “Marble is stone that expects to be admired after suffering tools.”

name: quartzite rocks
shortDescription: Quartzite rocks with a hard sparkle and a talent for resisting easy work. They serve builders, grinders, and decorative cutters who want stone that catches light without surrendering strength.
flavorText: “Quartzite shines just enough to remind masons it is still winning.”

name: sandstone boulder
shortDescription: A sandstone boulder with warm color, visible grain, and enough bulk to become blocks, carvings, steps, or a regrettable hauling contract. It is softer than granite but still heavy enough to humble careless planning.
flavorText: “Sandstone remembers the desert, even when dragged into town.”

name: sandstone pebbles
shortDescription: Sandstone pebbles in warm earthy tones, useful for paths, garden beds, drainage, and small decorative work. They are modest cargo, but a good courtyard can make even pebbles look like intentional luxury.
flavorText: “A pebble becomes refined when someone arranges enough of them.”

name: sandstone rocks
shortDescription: Sandstone rocks that cut more kindly than harder stone while still carrying respectable strength. Builders like them for walls, steps, and carved trim, especially in dry towns where their warm color suits the dust.
flavorText: “Easy to shape does not mean easy to carry.”

name: shale boulder
shortDescription: A shale boulder layered with thin plates and a stubborn habit of splitting along its own opinions. It is useful for rough building, fill, and workshop needs, though every handler learns to respect its sharp edges.
flavorText: “Shale breaks cleanly only when it feels like cooperating.”

name: shale pebbles
shortDescription: Shale pebbles, flat-edged and dark, gathered for paths, drainage beds, and practical ground cover. They crunch under boots with a sound that makes courtyards feel orderly and warehouses feel only slightly less muddy.
flavorText: “Even mud respects a good layer of stone.”

name: shale rocks
shortDescription: Shale rocks with layered faces and a dark, workmanlike look. They are not showy stone, but roofers, builders, and yard masters keep finding uses for them whenever cheaper material would become trouble later.
flavorText: “Shale is what happens when mud learns patience and sharp edges.”

name: coal
shortDescription: A chunk of coal, black, dusty, and full of stored heat. Smithies, kilns, kitchens, and cold houses all depend on it, which gives this ugly cargo a stronger market than many prettier things ever enjoy.
flavorText: “Coal is night that agreed to burn.”

name: anvil
shortDescription: A solid anvil with a scarred face, steady horn, and the quiet authority of a tool that has shaped thousands of louder tools. It is expensive to move, impossible to fake, and the center of any forge worth trusting.
flavorText: “Every blade begins by losing an argument with an anvil.”

name: bait
shortDescription: A packet of bait prepared for hooks, traps, and impatient fishers who prefer not to discuss luck. It smells convincing, stores poorly if forgotten, and sells best at dawn when hope is awake and breakfast is not.
flavorText: “Bait is a lie told in a language fish understand.”

name: plank
shortDescription: A single wooden plank, trimmed for repairs, building, patchwork, docks, crates, wagons, and all the small emergencies that begin with a crack. It is simple stock, but no carpenter ever complains about having one too many.
flavorText: “A plank is a solution waiting for nails.”

name: boat
shortDescription: A small trade boat suited to river crossings, harbor errands, fishing lines, and smugglers with modest ambitions. Its worth depends on watertight seams, local currents, and whether the buyer asks about storms before or after paying.
flavorText: “A boat is a wooden argument against staying where you are.”

name: bridge
shortDescription: A portable bridge section or sturdy crossing frame meant for ditches, streams, broken roads, and temporary work sites. It sells to engineers, armies, caravans, and anyone who has learned that going around can cost more than carrying across.
flavorText: “A bridge turns an obstacle into a toll opportunity.”

name: bucket
shortDescription: A sturdy bucket with a useful handle and a life full of water, sand, grain, dye, ash, and everything else people move in small wet or dry portions. It is too common for praise and too necessary to ignore.
flavorText: “A bucket proves usefulness does not need elegance.”

name: fire wood
shortDescription: Split fire wood, dry enough to catch and plain enough to be sold by the bundle without ceremony. Inns, kitchens, camps, kilns, and cold travelers all treat it as ordinary until the night grows dark and prices grow persuasive.
flavorText: “Firewood is cheaper before sunset.”

name: hammer
shortDescription: A basic hammer with a worn grip, useful weight, and enough dents to prove it has settled many small disputes between hands and materials. Builders, smiths, coopers, and improvised repairers all understand it without needing instructions.
flavorText: “Some problems become simpler after being struck correctly.”

name: strong hammer
shortDescription: A strong hammer built heavier than the common sort, made for stubborn pegs, thick nails, forging work, and jobs where hesitation only bruises the worker. It is not subtle, but subtlety rarely drives iron into timber.
flavorText: “The handle asks nicely. The head does not.”

name: hand axe
shortDescription: A hand axe with a compact head, firm grip, and enough bite for camp work, trimming, rough carpentry, and splitting smaller wood. Travelers like it because it is a tool first and a warning second, which is a useful order on bad roads.
flavorText: “A small axe turns branches into plans.”

name: ink and quill
shortDescription: A bottle of ink with a trimmed quill, ready for ledgers, contracts, letters, maps, and all the trouble words can cause when properly recorded. Merchants value it because a written debt has sharper teeth than a spoken promise.
flavorText: “Ink is the quietest way to bind a loud argument.”

name: label
shortDescription: A plain label meant for jars, crates, shelves, sacks, and the fragile hope that someone will know what a thing is later. It is cheap paper until it prevents the wrong powder from entering the wrong stew.
flavorText: “A label is a small mercy offered to future confusion.”

name: ladder
shortDescription: A wooden ladder with steady rungs and the usual promise of reaching places that seemed easier from the ground. Builders, orchard keepers, warehouse hands, and reckless apprentices all need one, though not always wisely.
flavorText: “Every ladder sells confidence by the rung.”
